Article Title: How to get your CIBIL credit score without a PAN card
Article Text:
Your credit score is a three-digit number that reflects your creditworthiness and repayment capacity.

What is a CIBIL score?

How to get your CIBIL Credit score without a PAN Card

Factors affecting your CIBIL score

CIBIL credit score : Understanding your creditworthiness is pivotal in today's financial landscape, especially when seeking loans or credit cards. The Credit Information Bureau (India) Limited, commonly known as CIBIL, plays an important role in this regard by providing credit scores to borrowers.According to ET, credit score helps banks make informed decisions about extending credit cards and loans. Your credit history influences your CIBIL score , which is calculated using a special method.Your CIBIL score is a three-digit number that reflects your creditworthiness and repayment capacity. Ranging from 300 to 900, a higher score indicates a stronger likelihood of loan approval. Banks and financial institutions use this score to assess the risk associated with lending money.1. Go to the official CIBIL website and click on 'Personal CIBIL score,' then 'Get Your Free CIBIL score.' Follow the link provided and fill in all the required details.2. If you don’t have a PAN Card, you can use an alternative identification such as Passport, Voter ID, Driver’s License, or Ration Card. Enter the number of the chosen ID.3. Provide your date of birth, PIN code, and select your state. Finally, enter your mobile number and click on ‘Accept and Continue’.4. Authenticate your identity by entering the OTP (One-Time Password) delivered to your mobile device on the next screen. Click 'Continue' to proceed.5. Choose whether you want to link the device with your account. Select 'Yes' or 'No' based on your preference.6. Upon successful registration, you will see a page confirming that 'You have successfully registered!'7. Click on ‘Go to Dashboard’ to access and view your CIBIL score.The factors influencing the new CIBIL score, as stated on the CIBIL website, include:1. Depth of credit: This refers to the duration of your existing credit history, calculated from the opening date of your oldest credit account.2. Long-term trend of outstanding balances: The overall trend of outstanding balances over time affects your score.3. Transaction history on credit cards: How you manage transactions on your credit cards impacts your score.4. Ratio of actual repayment amount to total amount due: This ratio indicates your repayment behavior and influences your score accordingly.5. Number of new accounts opened and number of accounts closed: Opening or closing accounts can affect your score, so managing these actions wisely is important.
Article Summary: Your CIBIL score is a three-digit number that reflects your creditworthiness and repayment capacity. Ranging from 300 to 900, a higher score indicates a stronger likelihood of loan approval. Banks and financial institutions use this score to assess the risk associated with lending money.

Article Title: Airlines turn to AI to save fuel; IndiGo to start trials for thriftier take-offs from today
Article Text:
Airlines turn to AI to save fuel; IndiGo to start trials for thriftier take-offs

NEW DELHI: After doing everything humanely possible to cut fuel burn, Indian airlines have turned to artificial intelligence (AI) to help save the prohibitively expensive black gold. IndiGo has asked its pilots to save fuel during the time when the aircraft is most thirsty - climb which is done full throttle (car equivalent of the gas pedal being floored) - by opting for “OptiClimb solution” starting Thursday on trial basis on its Airbus fleet.In simple terms, instead of taking off at a constant speed, they will do so at varying speeds wherever possible. The airline aims to save 60-65 kilo per take off using this AI solution. Multiplying that with its 2,000-odd flights, the saving could be impressive even if half of them use this. Lower fuel burn means lower emissions too. Depending on the outcome of the trial period flights, the airline will decide on making it a regular feature on its Airbus fleet.“Over the past several months, we have been working closely with SITA (world's leading flight operation solution provider) on the implementation of their OptiClimb solution (which is) an AI-powered flight optimisation solution that helps pilots reduce fuel burn and carbon emissions during the energy-intensive climb phase of a flight,” a mail sent by Captain Ashim Mittra, IndiGo’s senior VP (flight operations), to its about 6,000 pilots on Wednesday night says.“The climb phase typically accounts for the highest rate of fuel consumption during any flight…. Recent advancements in aircraft performance analytics have enabled more refined climb optimisation techniques…. By considering actual aircraft performance, planned take-off weight, cruise altitude, and prevailing atmospheric conditions, OptiClimb enables more efficient climb profiles, delivering measurable reductions in fuel consumption and associated carbon emissions while maintaining the highest standards of operational safety and regulatory compliance,” the mail adds.In contemporary times, fuel saving has become a necessity since the 2008 subprime crisis when global economy was slowing down amid energy prices shooting up. At that time, airlines made aircraft lighter by stripping away non essential onboard things like curtains, partitions, carrying less water. More fuel efficient engines were developed over time. The next gen of fuel efficient aircraft using completely different engine architecture is in the works but is at least a decade away if not more. Till they become a reality, AI could help airlines save some fuel.“The primary objective of this (OptiClimb) initiative is to enhance fuel efficiency, reduce our carbon footprint, and strengthen operational sustainability, while ensuring full adherence to Air Traffic Control (ATC) requirements and safe operating practices. As airlines worldwide continue to focus on operational excellence and environmental stewardship, the adoption of data-driven solutions such as OptiClimb demonstrates how advanced analytics can unlock incremental efficiencies beyond those achievable through conventional Flight Management System (FMS) optimisation alone,” Captain Mittra’s email adds.
Article Summary: IndiGo has asked its pilots to save fuel during the time when the aircraft is most thirsty - climb. The airline aims to save 60-65 kilo per take off using this AI solution. Depending on the outcome of the trial period flights, the airline will decide on making it a regular feature on its Airbus fleet.

Article Title: Summer travel hack: How to use your credit card to save big
Article Text:
Aligning travelling preferences and plans with the right credit card ensures that the benefits align with your actual spending. (AI image)

Evaluate Core Benefits on a Travel Credit Card

Reward Earning Rate:

Credit card for travel: 4 things to check

Redemption Value:

Transfer Partners:

Terms and Conditions:

Understand Your Travel Preferences Before Choosing a Card

One fee can increase travel cost

Look Beyond Rewards, Consider Travel Perks that Matter

Save on International Travel with Zero Forex Mark-up Cards



Use Wisely, Maximize Value from Your Travel Credit Card

Understand various layers

(The author, Santosh Agarwal is the CEO of Paisabazaar)

Summer is peak travel season with high demand leading to higher costs, and travellers actively looking for smart ways to save on flight and hotel bookings. While travel deals, seasonal discounts and booking offers can help reduce costs to an extent, the right credit cards can go a long way in maximizing savings on travel spends. From free flight tickets and hotel stays to lounge access and accelerated rewards on travel bookings, smart planning and the right credit card can significantly reduce overall travel expenses when used smartly.When selecting a travel credit card, you should focus on the overall value proposition instead of the headline benefits. Ideally, core benefits on travel-centric cards should be evaluated across four major parameters - reward earning rate, redemption value, transfer partners and associated terms and conditions.The key thing to know is how many points or miles you will earn through your spending across different categories. While most credit cards offer a decent base reward rate, the real value often comes from accelerated earning on travel and other bonus categories.Airline or portal co-branded cards typically offer higher earnings on their associated platforms. Many premium and super-premium cards provide significantly higher returns on travel bookings made through their own reward marketplaces.Moreover, several cards offer bonus rewards or travel vouchers on reaching spending milestones which can again help high-spending travellers maximize returns from their cards.Hence, travellers should carefully evaluate where and how they intend to use the card to maximize earnings.Some cards may offer higher reward earning rate leading to quicker point accumulation but if the redemption value is low, effective returns will not be good. Hence, checking the redemption value is equally important. Travellers must assess the value that each point delivers when redeeming them against flights, hotels or other travel benefits.The utility of a travel card for you depends on its list of transfer partners. A card may offer excellent value-back on travel but if the partner airlines or hotels do not align with your travel preferences, the benefits may remain underutilized. Hence, always check the transfer partners and the point-to-mile conversion ratio for your preferred partner before selecting a card.While the above three core parameters are the most important, each may come with associated terms and conditions, directly affecting the actual savings. Most cards cap total reward earnings in the accelerated categories, which could be a key factor for high-spenders looking to maximize value. Some cards also put restrictions on the points that you can redeem. Similarly, some cards may have slightly complex redemption structures.A credit card that works well for a frequent international traveller may not necessarily be the best fit for someone who takes domestic vacations. So, instead of applying for the most popular travel card in the market, you should first evaluate your travel preferences and choose a card that can provide the highest value.For instance, travellers who prefer flying with a particular carrier may benefit more from airline co-branded credit cards. These cards generally offer accelerated earning in the form of the airline’s loyalty currency or air miles, along with benefits such as priority boarding, discounted tickets or complimentary flight vouchers. On the other hand, those who want flexibility should consider premium travel cards that allow reward point transfers across multiple airline and hotel partners.Aligning travelling preferences and plans with the right credit card ensures that the benefits align with your actual spending.While reward earning, redemption value and transfer partners determine the core value proposition of a travel card, additional travel perks can further enhance the overall experience and savings for frequent travellers.Complimentary domestic and international airport lounge access remains one of the most popular benefits sought by frequent flyers. Most cards offer a limited number of visits per quarter for primary users, whereas premium and super-premium cards may provide unlimited access, often extended to add-on card users as well.Some premium travel-centric cards also come with complimentary hotel memberships, room upgrades, airport meet and greet and priority concierge services, making the overall travel experience much more convenient for you.While these additional perks do deliver meaningful value, you must assess whether they will genuinely utilize these benefits. For instance, getting a high-fee super-premium card only for a complimentary meet and greet once a year may not be the smartest idea. Instead, you should first focus on the core benefits and choose a card that provides the right balance of value-back, meaningful perks and manageable annual costs.Most credit cards charge up to 3.5% forex mark-up fee when you make a transaction in a foreign currency. This increases the overall cost of foreign travel and can quietly become a major expense for frequent international travellers.Several travel-focused cards come with low or zero forex mark-up benefits. Those who are planning overseas vacations this summer should also consider forex mark-up charges carefully in addition to rewards and additional travel perks.Simply owning a travel credit card is not enough. The real savings come from using the card strategically. Since most travel cards offer accelerated value-back on travel expenses, cardholders should try to route major travel expenses such as flight tickets, hotel bookings and holiday packages through the right card. Card issuers also run limited period offers across online travel portals, airlines or hotels which can further increase savings.When it comes to redemption, you should check all available options and their point-to-value ratio and then redeem wisely. Most cards would provide better value if you choose point-to-mile conversion with partner airlines or hotels than direct cashback or vouchers. Additionally, using lounge access, complimentary memberships and travel privileges regularly can substantially improve the effective value derived from the card.While most travel cards are designed to reward high-spenders handsomely, you should keep an eye on their expenditure and spend only as much as they can afford to pay back on time. Overspending merely to achieve spending milestones or unlock additional perks can potentially lead to debt.As summer travel continues to rise, choosing the right travel credit card can make a significant difference to the overall travel budget. However, the key is not just selecting a popular card but in understanding how the rewards work, where the real value lies and which benefits genuinely align with your travel habits.
Article Summary: Summer is peak travel season with high demand leading to higher costs. Travellers are looking for smart ways to save on flight and hotel bookings. The right credit cards can go a long way in maximizing savings on travel spends. From free flight tickets and hotel stays to lounge access and accelerated rewards on travel bookings, smart planning can significantly reduce overall travel expenses.

Thinking about dental implants abroad in 2026? Turkey almost certainly belongs on your shortlist. The same premium implant systems used across Western Europe are available here at 60–70% lower prices, fitted by internationally trained specialists in clinics built around international patients. This guide explains why Turkey leads the market, how pricing works, and how to tell serious operators from informal ones, before ranking the ten clinics setting the standard this year.Vera Smile is widely regarded as one of top dental implant clinics in Turkey , backed by their American Academy of Cosmetic Dentistry (AACD) recognition and Slow Dentistry® membership. As a sub-brand of Vera Clinic (one of Turkey's most internationally recognised medical groups), it benefits from the group's R&D infrastructure, including Vera Academy, which develops next-generation technologies such as Vector-10™.Treatments use premium systems (Straumann and Nobel Biocare) supported by Digital Smile Design and CAD/CAM workflows, covering the full spectrum from single and multiple implants to All-on-4 and All-on-6 full-arch solutions, with transparent pricing: implants from €450, crowns from €200, veneers from €275. Vera Smile uses Hiossen IMP, NDI Medical, Straumann, J Dental Care, and Neodent implants, and is in high demand among international patients, so booking well in advance is the safest way to secure your preferred treatment window.Kristal Clinic offers 3D-guided placement, CAD/CAM prosthetic design, and digital smile mock-ups before any procedure begins. They also offer multilingual coordinators, VIP transfers, and full travel coordination support for international patients. Implants range from €470, crowns from €210, and veneers from €280. They use Straumann, Nobel Biocare, Hiossen, Medigma, IRES, BEGO, Venus, and DTI. Requesting the full 3D plan before booking flights is worthwhile, since pre-arrival planning is a key strength.A specialist team reports 96.5% success for full-arch cases, using Straumann and Nobel Biocare with 3D CBCT imaging, CAD/CAM, and laser dentistry. It includes a lifetime implant guarantee, 5-year crown warranty, and same-day protocols for selected full-arch cases, plus support in eight languages. They use Straumann, Nobel Biocare, and Medigma. The same-day protocol can compress treatment into a single visit.WestDent Clinic reports a 95%+ success rate with detailed digital planning. Eight treatment rooms with extended hours, a full implant portfolio plus orthodontics and gum surgery, and CAD/CAM milling. Implants from €500, crowns from €210, veneers from €290. They use Straumann (Official Provider), Nobel Biocare, MODE, NTA, Bicon, Osstem, and Medentika. Its pre-treatment mapping is among the most detailed in the region.The clinic is known for advanced surgical work: All-on-4/6, sinus lifts, and bone grafting, with European-standard sterilisation. It offers zirconia crowns, veneers, and long-term warranties, including lifetime guarantees on select cases. Implants from €450, crowns from €200, All-on-4 from €3,500. Dentavrasya uses FDA-approved systems, though brands aren't publicly named. For bone loss or full-mouth cases, asking about the grafting and All-on-4 history is where its expertise shows most.Founded in 2013, the clinic is Health Tourism certified by the Turkish Ministry of Health. It specialises in zirconia crowns, veneers, Hollywood Smile, and implant restorations, with personalised digital plans prepared before travel. Implants from €480, crowns from €200, veneers from €275. They use titanium-based implants (brands not named). Sharing x-rays in advance lets Dentevim build a full plan before you arrive.Dentatur is a dual-location clinic offering all-inclusive packages covering treatment, hotel, and VIP transfers, with in-house labs enabling same-week restorations. They have a strong UK and German patient base. Implants from €450, crowns from €180, veneers from €250. Dentatur uses Straumann, Nobel Biocare, and Astra Tech. Confirming inclusions before booking matters, since accommodation and transfers vary between branches.Established in 2008 on the Aegean coast, Marmaris Dental Center is popular with UK and Scandinavian patients. Oral surgeons and prosthodontists here handle implants and restorative work using CE-certified materials, with plans built around short trips. Implant prices start from €500, crowns from €220, and veneers from €280. Uses Nobel Biocare, Straumann, and Alpha Bio. The coastal setting suits the rest period between placement and final restoration.Alanya Dental Clinic is one of the Riviera's longest-standing clinics, founded in 1993, with ISO-certified sterilisation and three decades of international experience. Its on-site lab enables 5–7 day timelines. Implant prices start from €480, crowns from €200, and veneers from €270. Alanya Dental Clinic uses a mix of foreign premium and Turkish brands (not disclosed). The quick turnaround pairs easily with a holiday in Alanya.EsteDent Turkey is reputed for smile-led work, including implant-supported Hollywood Smile cases. It specialises in E-max veneers, zirconia crowns, gum contouring, and whitening, using minimally invasive techniques and advanced imaging. It’s popular with younger patients. Veneer prices start from €270, crowns from €200, and whitening from €180. Its implant brand has not been disclosed. Its before-and-after galleries give the clearest read on its aesthetic style.Turkey hosts over a million international patients annually, with dentistry among its most in-demand specialities. Internationally trained specialists, direct flights, and state-backed infrastructure coordinated by USHAS make it a natural hub. Cities like Istanbul, Izmir, Antalya, and Alanya host hundreds of clinics offering multilingual coordinators, hotel partnerships, and treatments streamlined for one- or two-week trips for dental implant treatments in Turkey The significant price difference for the same premium implant system is due to Turkey's lower operational overhead, government support (tax incentives and VAT exemptions), and favourable exchange rates, which keep margins lean compared to Western countries.The selection process relies on five checks: verifying accreditations (MoH license, ISO 9001, AACD/ITI), material transparency (named implant/crown brands), clear, itemised pricing, verifiable dentist credentials, and positive independent reviews. A clinic scoring well on all five is the safest option.
Article Summary: Vera Smile is widely regarded as one of top dental implant clinics in Turkey. The same premium implant systems used across Western Europe are available here at 60–70% lower prices. This guide explains why Turkey leads the market, how pricing works, and how to tell serious operators from informal ones.

Article Title: In 2014, Ian Goodfellow introduced competing neural nets that power modern AI image tools
Article Text:
StyleGAN2 This person does not actually exist. This image is of a fictitious person created by a generative adversarial network of type. Image Credit: Wikimedia Commons

Creative competition between two machines

Why 2014 was the breakthrough year

A photo of American computer scientist Ian Goodfellow in Mountain View, California. Image Credit: Wikimedia Commons

Overcoming the limitations of the code

In 2014, Ian Goodfellow proposed a concept that became influential in artificial intelligence. His notion revolved around two neural networks, which were computer structures designed like human brains, engaged in a never-ending battle against each other. One of the neural networks was responsible for creating images of its own imagination, and another one was trying to detect any lies. All of this was called a generative adversarial network, or GAN. This technique offered a new way for machines to improve through adversarial training. The concept is widely seen as an important milestone in modern image generation.At the heart of the invention of GAN lay a remarkably straightforward concept, considering the enormous implications it had. Rather than having humans develop detailed instructions for the machine to follow, the system learned through direct competition. The first model, called a generator, acts like a digital art forger trying to create convincing fakes. The second model, known as a discriminator, is an art detective that can spot the forgery among real images. This kind of rivalry results in internal motivation for improvement, with each failure of the detective serving as learning material for the forger. As a 2025 paper cited in PubMed indicates, the two-model architecture has become the common way to explain the functioning of GANs.This approach does not require the generator to understand image meaning in a human sense. It just needed to keep getting better at deceiving its opponent. Thus, the technique proved extremely helpful in creating realistic pictures regardless of the lack of training data available for the research. Over time, researchers found that the approach could be used for image-to-image translation, image enhancement, and synthetic data generation.Before Goodfellow's proposal, the development of deep learning was rapid, but computers were unable to produce believable pictures. The main problem with those earlier systems was that there was no way to refine their output. Generative Adversarial Networks provided a new solution to this challenge through the development of a gaming paradigm. In a 2026 review paper published in Nature , the researchers describe how the early versions of the networks developed the necessary framework for image generation techniques.Although more advanced technology has appeared since then, the idea continues to be an important landmark. The process was aimed at improving realism. It was due to the continuous struggle between the digital detective and the digital forger that the images produced became more realistic quickly. To cope with such challenges, the AI had to pay attention to small details.Nevertheless, GANs had many flaws that led to a new generation of AI development. Thus, according to the same review article published in Nature in 2026, initial GANs were often unstable in training and were plagued by the problem of mode collapsing. It occurs when the generator learns to produce one image that can fool the discriminator and repeats it endlessly.None of those shortcomings could negate the significance of the innovation. On the contrary, they helped researchers understand precisely where work was needed further on. After it became clear that it was possible to generate images realistically using GANs, other researchers were able to come up with new solutions, such as diffusion models, to overcome the issues of instability and low-quality results.Overall, the role of the 2014 proposal should be considered as that of a fantastic start instead of an ultimate conclusion. With his brilliant insight into the conflict of two computer programs, Ian Goodfellow managed to turn the complicated engineering task into a game. It was not just an innovative technological invention but also a breakthrough in terms of the philosophy of technology.
Article Summary: In 2014, computer scientist Ian Goodfellow proposed a concept that became influential in artificial intelligence. His notion revolved around two neural networks engaged in a never-ending battle against each other. One of the neural networks was responsible for creating images of its own imagination, and another one was trying to detect any lies. This technique offered a new way for machines to improve through adversarial training.

Article Title: In 1994, Denso engineers redesigning production labels unexpectedly invented the modern QR code
Article Text:
Masahiro Hara QR code's father honored by French artist qargo. Image Credit: Wikimedia Commons

An industry stuck in 1974: why are we still using 1D barcodes?

Why a square grid changed everything

Scan the QR Code to unlock instant information and access. Image Credit: Wikimedia Commons

Built to survive the harsh reality of manufacturing

From car parts to a global standard

Today, the quick response code widely known as the QR code. We see it on restaurant tables to view menus, on digital tickets for concerts, and on smartphone screens to make quick payments. Because it is so common in our daily lives, it is easy to assume that it was invented by tech companies for modern smartphones. However, the true history of this black and white square is far less glamorous. It was developed out of frustration on a busy manufacturing floor in Japan.In 1994, engineers at a company called Denso Wave, which was a major parts supplier for the automotive giant Toyota, were facing a massive daily problem. The workers on the production line were struggling to keep track of thousands of car components moving through the factory. The old tools they relied on were simply failing to keep up with the speed of modern car manufacturing.Before the QR code existed, factories relied heavily on standard, one-dimensional barcodes. These are the familiar rows of vertical lines that you still see on grocery items today.While traditional barcodes worked well for simple tasks, they had severe technical limitations. A standard barcode can only hold about twenty alphanumeric characters of data.For the automotive workers at Denso Wave, twenty characters were not nearly enough. To track a single car part accurately, the label needed to include the part number, the batch details, the shipping destination, and manufacturing dates. Because a single barcode could not hold this much data, workers had to print several barcodes on a single part label. On the factory floor, employees spent a significant amount of time scanning up to fifteen separate barcodes on just one box of components. This process slowed production lines and caused wrist strain from repetitive scanning.To solve this production bottleneck, an engineer named Masahiro Hara led a small team to create a completely new type of tracking symbol. A research paper published by the University of Cincinnati states that the team decided to design a two-dimensional matrix code. This meant that instead of storing data in just one horizontal line of bars, the new code would store data both horizontally and vertically across a square grid.By utilizing two dimensions, the new design could hold several thousand characters of information in the exact same amount of physical space. In fact, it could store up to two hundred times more data than a standard barcode. This breakthrough meant that a single scan could reveal the key information a factory worker needed about a specific car part, reducing the need to scan multiple labels on a single box.A factory floor is a notoriously difficult environment for technology. Labels frequently get wiped with oil, covered in dust, or physically torn as parts are moved around by heavy machinery. Standard barcodes become completely unreadable if even a small part of their vertical lines is scratched or smudged.The engineers at Denso Wave designed the QR code to be durable. According to an industry analysis by the Polytechnic University of Catalonia , the matrix design allows the code to retain its data even when it is dirty or damaged. The code uses error correction technology, which means that up to thirty percent of the square can be destroyed or obscured and still be readable by a scanner. Furthermore, the distinct finder patterns in three corners of the code allow scanners to read it from any angle, whether it is upside down or tilted.What began as a clever piece of industrial engineering quickly outgrew the walls of the automotive assembly plant. Denso Wave made a highly unusual and generous decision at the time: they chose not to exercise their patent rights on the invention. They made the technology completely free for anyone in the world to use.This open access allowed the square code to slowly migrate from manufacturing plants into shipping logistics, retail, and eventually into the consumer market. When smartphones with built-in cameras became popular, the public suddenly had the power to read these codes instantly without any specialist industrial equipment. The simple square grid, originally built to survive oil stains and track car parts, became a bridge between the physical world and the digital one.
Article Summary: In 1994, engineers at a company called Denso Wave, which was a major parts supplier for the automotive giant Toyota, were facing a massive daily problem. The workers on the production line were struggling to keep track of thousands of car components moving through the factory. To solve this production bottleneck, an engineer named Masahiro Hara led a small team to create a new type of tracking symbol.

Article Title: In 1990, engineer Tim Berners-Lee developed a CERN browser that transformed online information access
Article Text:
Sir Timothy John Berners-Lee (born 8 June 1955), also known as TimBL, is an English computer scientist best known as the inventor of the World Wide Web, HTML, the URL system, and HTTP. Image Credit: Wikimedia Commons

A practical solution to the laboratory problem

NeXT workstation (NeXTcube and monitor) used by Tim Berners-Lee at CERN as the first Web server on the World Wide Web. Image Credit: Wikimedia Commons

Why the browser changed everything

A

research tool

is becoming an everyday activity

Clicking on links, browsing pages, and exploring digital space is a matter of course for us now. But it did not happen by chance, and it was not simply the inevitable result of technological progress. In 1990, software engineer Tim Berners-Lee took the first steps on a project that would transform how people interact with information. He developed the project at CERN, the European Organisation for Nuclear Research.In reality, the World Wide Web was created not to build something new and unique, but to solve a practical problem within the research organization. As can be seen from the historical account of the Internet and the World Wide Web found on PubMed , the researchers of CERN have faced a great difficulty in sharing their documents and information about projects among different laboratories and departments.That timing is important for understanding the topic. This shows that the web browser did not emerge fully formed. Rather, it emerged from the need to connect documents and researchers within a readable network. In addition, the project itself was not aimed at creating a commercial product. It was meant to make the network easier for anyone in the laboratory to use.In order to make this new network possible, it became necessary to alter the documents themselves. Another peer-reviewed article on the history of computer languages explains how the foundation was laid. According to the article devoted to the history of SGML and XML languages, published on PubMed , HyperText Markup Language, better known as HTML, appeared at CERN in 1989.The development of HTML brought yet another important level of historical context to the browser itself. A browser is of limited use if the documents it opens do not connect. Unlike standalone computer files, HTML allowed one document to link to another. Moving between different pieces of information then became seamless. The web took shape in a way that users could navigate without understanding the underlying computer systems.Now that the language was ready, the system needed a way to access it. That way was provided by the browser itself. Historical accounts say the first text-based browser was developed in 1991, following the initial work in 1990. The browser was an integral part of the system, not an optional extra. Once users could open linked documents in the same interface, the web became a practical way to manage information.Before this, digital information was often stored in isolated systems. To access files, users often had to navigate separate systems and learn complex command structures. Hyperlinks changed this by creating a clear route from one webpage to another. Because HTML was publicly accessible, it spread quickly across the World Wide Web. Browsers provided access to web pages, while HTML supplied their content. The invention created a system that allowed people not only to store information but also to navigate it easily.One notable feature of the first browser was how simple it was. Being absolutely text-based, it had none of the sophisticated visual layouts, images, and other graphical elements that we see today. Yet that simplicity became an important advantage.Ultimately, the Internet became widely adopted because it made information feel more accessible. The change was not only about the application itself, but also about teaching people a new way to think about information. Web browsing turns reading into navigation. By turning a research tool into something routine, the early Internet helped create a more open and connected web.
Article Summary: Tim Berners-Lee is best known as the inventor of the World Wide Web, HTML, the URL system, and HTTP. He developed the project at CERN, the European Organisation for Nuclear Research. The web emerged from the need to connect documents and researchers within a readable network.

Article Title: India leads Asia Pacific in AI use: 60% of consumers want their own personal AI agents, says Adobe
Article Text:
Adobe representative image

AI is redefining the Indian shopping journey



65% use AI to search for personalized product recommendations.

62% are open to using a virtual AI concierge to handle their shopping.

60% rely on AI for instant customer service and troubleshooting.

The expectation gap



76% of consumers demand that AI-driven interactions feel human rather than robotic.

61% say they would completely stop doing business with a brand if they discovered they were talking to an AI when they expected a human.

Key Reassurances: Clear labeling that a tool is AI (21%) and the ability to instantly hand off the conversation to a human representative (17%) are the top demands for building consumer trust.

Indian consumers are showing the strongest appetite for advanced artificial intelligence (AI) in the Asia Pacific region, with a staggering 60% expressing a desire to build their own personal AI agents, a report has said. The findings, published on June 25 in the 16th annual Adobe AI and Digital Trends Report, reveal that India is overtaking both neighboring markets and local corporate expectations when it comes to embracing “agentic AI”, which are systems capable of executing complex tasks autonomously.The data highlights a cultural readiness for automated interactions. More than half of Indian consumers (55%) say they would happily interact with a brand’s AI agent, the report said, adding that 58% are comfortable with “agent-to-agent” interactions. Furthermore, 61% are perfectly comfortable letting an AI agent deal with a brand’s human representative on their behalf.Indian shoppers are increasingly integrating smart tools into their daily purchasing habits, looking for speed, convenience, and deep personalization. Adobe report says:“India is emerging as one of the most receptive markets for agentic AI in Asia Pacific, creating new opportunities for brands to deliver more personalised and effective customer experiences.At the same time, consumers expect these interactions to be transparent, intuitive and human-centric,” said Anindita Veluri, Director of Marketing at Adobe India.Despite high consumer enthusiasm, the report points to a disconnect between what everyday people want from AI and how companies measure its success. While brands remain focused on slashing costs and boosting internal efficiency metrics, consumers judge an AI's success on trust, transparency, and whether their needs are actually being met.“Many consumers are comfortable with agentic AI, but say adoption relies on defined transparent contexts with options for human support,” explained Duncan Egan, Adobe’s Vice President of Enterprise Marketing for Asia Pacific and Japan.The report notes that human connection still dictates whether a consumer stays or leaves:
Article Summary: More than half of Indian consumers (55%) say they would happily interact with a brand’s AI agent. 58% are comfortable with ‘agent-to-agent’ interactions. 61% are perfectly comfortable letting an AI agent deal on their behalf. Indian shoppers increasingly integrating smart tools into their daily purchasing habits.

Article Title: IBM unveils world’s first technology for AI chips smaller than 1 nanometer
Article Text:
Breaking the boundaries of Moore’s Law



‘Nanostack’ 3D architecture: How IBM achieved this milestone



IBM has unveiled the world’s first technology capable of manufacturing microchips smaller than 1 nanometer. This comes as tech giants race to build more powerful hardware capable of handling increasingly massive artificial intelligence (AI) workloads.For decades, chipmakers have tried to squeeze more computing power into smaller spaces – a trend known as Moore’s Law. IBM’s new 0.7-nanometer (or 7 angstrom) architecture positions the company to compete with contract manufacturing giants like Intel and TSMC, as per a report by news agency Reuters.To put the achievement in perspective, Intel just recently announced that its 1.8-nanometer process had entered “risk production” testing. IBM's new technology shrinks components even further, packing nearly 100 billion transistors onto a surface the size of a fingernail. Compared to IBM’s milestone 2-nanometer chip released in 2021, this new architecture is claimed to offer 50% higher computing performance or 70% greater energy efficiency.To achieve sub-1-nanometer sizes, IBM developed a proprietary transistor design called “nanostack”. Instead of laying transistors flat next to each other on a chip, the nanostack design layers them vertically in three dimensions.“With our new nanostack architecture, we’re not just making smaller transistors, we’re reinventing how chips are built to deliver dramatically more power and energy efficiency,” said Jay Gambetta, director of IBM Research.Beyond raw processing power, the nanostack technology tackles a critical bottleneck in AI computing: memory. The new process shrinks static random-access memory (SRAM) circuits by a massive 40%. This specific type of memory is highly coveted by the AI industry, and is utilised in Nvidia's new Groq chips and hardware from Cerebras Systems – both of whom currently rely entirely on TSMC for manufacturing.IBM estimates that commercial production using this technology could begin within the next five years. While IBM designs these cutting-edge blueprints, it does not operate its own commercial factories. The company has previously licensed its chip innovations to global manufacturers like Samsung and Japan's Rapidus.
Article Summary: IBM has unveiled the world’s first technology capable of manufacturing microchips smaller than 1 nanometer. This comes as tech giants race to build more powerful hardware capable of handling increasingly massive artificial intelligence (AI) workloads. The new architecture is claimed to offer 50% higher computing performance or 70% greater energy efficiency.

Article Title: Apple hikes iPad, Mac prices in India as AI-driven memory crunch bites; MacBook Pro cost jumps Rs 70,000
Article Text:
The base 14-inch MacBook Pro with the M5 chip and 16GB RAM, launched at Rs 1,69,900, is now priced at Rs 2,39,900 — a jump of Rs 70,000.

MacBook Air with the M5 chip has gone from Rs 1,19,900 to Rs 1,49,900,

15-inch variant now costs Rs 1,79,900, up Rs 35,000 from its launch price of Rs 1,44,900. Apple’s entry-level MacBook Neo has also become Rs 10,000 more expensive.

iPad Pro now starts at Rs 1,39,900, the iPad Air at Rs 89,900, the standard iPad at Rs 49,900 and the iPad mini at Rs 69,900.

Why Apple has increased prices of iPad and MacBooks



In a statement shared with TOI, Apple said the company had resisted passing on higher costs for as long as possible.

“

The consumer electronics industry is facing an unprecedented challenge. The rapid expansion of AI data centers has created an extraordinary surge in demand for memory and storage. We have never seen a component price increase this much, this quickly,

”
Article Summary: The base 14-inch MacBook Pro with the M5 chip and 16GB RAM, launched at Rs 1,69,900, is now priced at Rs 2,39,900 — a jump of Rs 70,000. Apple’s entry- level MacBook Neo has also become Rs 10,000 more expensive.

Article Title: In 1956, a Dartmouth workshop gave artificial intelligence its name and helped launch the field
Article Text:
John McCarthy, the computer scientist who organised the 1956 Dartmouth Summer Research Project on Artificial Intelligence. Image Credits Wikimedia Commons

A small workshop with an ambitious goal

Giving a name to a new scientific field

Dartmouth College in New Hampshire, where the 1956 workshop that gave artificial intelligence its name took place. Image Credits Wikimedia Commons

From one room to today's AI revolution

Long before artificial intelligence became the force behind chatbots, image generators and recommendation algorithms, it was little more than a collection of scattered ideas shared by mathematicians, computer scientists and philosophers. There was no established field called AI, no dedicated departments, and no common language to unite researchers pursuing the dream of thinking machines. That changed in the summer of 1956, when a small gathering at Dartmouth College helped transform a loose set of concepts into a recognised scientific discipline.According to The Meeting of the Minds That Launched AI , the Dartmouth conference was not an elaborate event but a small-scale meeting of academics. McCarthy and his fellow researchers felt that there were certain elements of learning and intelligent behaviour that could be precisely described so that a computer could mimic them. At the time, this was a highly radical idea because computing technology was still in its early daysThe significance of the meeting was not that it produced a working intelligent machine. Instead, it created a shared vision. By bringing together researchers from different disciplines, the workshop encouraged collaboration and helped establish a common set of questions. Could machines learn from experience? Could they solve problems independently? Could they process language in ways that resembled human thought? These questions became central to AI research for decades to come.What made the event particularly influential was the decision to give this emerging area of study a name.Scientific fields often develop gradually over many years, but the Dartmouth project provided a clear identity that researchers could rally around. The term "artificial intelligence" offered a label that was both ambitious and memorable, helping transform a collection of related ideas into a recognised discipline.Once “artificial intelligence” existed as a term, scattered ideas could be grouped under one banner. According to a study published on Artificial Intelligence , the beginning of modern AI research can be traced back to John McCarthy, who coined the term at Dartmouth College in 1956. The article treats that moment as the symbolic birth of the field.This new identity also made it easier for educational and research institutions, as well as funding agencies, to support this research. The recognition of AI as a genuine science meant that money was beginning to flow into the industry. Researchers could now present their work as part of a broader scientific effort rather than as a series of isolated tests. This, in turn, contributed to advances in expert systems, robotics, machine learning, and natural language processing.Earlier milestones, including theoretical work by Alan Turing and other pioneers, had already explored the possibility of machine intelligence. However, Dartmouth helped unify those ideas into a coherent discipline with a distinct identity and long-term research agenda.The workshop did not directly create the AI tools that millions of people use today. Modern technologies such as virtual assistants, recommendation systems, image generators and large language models are the result of decades of research, experimentation and technological advances. Yet the intellectual roots of these innovations can be traced back to the questions first organised under the banner of artificial intelligence in 1956.Years later, the Dartmouth meeting still reminds us that any big technological revolution starts not with an invention but with a good idea. The people who met at the Dartmouth summer workshop could not create the future during that summer, but they managed to develop a framework for the next generation to work within in order to reach the future. Their main accomplishment was not the construction of any machine or program, but creating a scientific domain where the study of intelligent machines became possible.AI now plays a role in various aspects of our lives, such as healthcare, education, finance, entertainment, and more. The Dartmouth Workshop provides a reminder that history is made not just through invention, but through occasions which give shared meaning to ideas.
Article Summary: Artificial intelligence was little more than a collection of ideas shared by mathematicians, computer scientists and philosophers. In the summer of 1956, a small gathering at Dartmouth College helped transform a loose set of concepts into a recognised scientific discipline. The significance of the meeting was not that it produced a working intelligent machine. Instead, it created a shared vision.
